messages.content: Plan the summer office party in london tomorrow

Okay, this is huge — we're throwing a London summer party tomorrow and I need my A-team on this. Pam handles the logistics, Angela makes sure we don't go bankrupt, and Kelly keeps everyone pumped about the vibe.

Oh wow, okay — London, tomorrow. I love the enthusiasm, and I really do want to make this happen for everyone, but I have to be honest with you: a same-day international event is... a lot. We'd be looking at flights, venue booking, catering, and getting the whole office coordinated across an ocean in under 24 hours. I want to be the "yes, and" person here, but I'd be doing you a disservice if I didn't flag that this timeline has some real challenges.

Here's what I can actually do: I can start pulling together a proper London party plan *right now* — venue options, a realistic budget range, a guest list, and a schedule — so that when we pick a real date, we're completely ready to go. Based on what happened with Michael's "live music experience" last year, I'd also really like to lock in the details before anything gets promised to the team. If we give people 2–3 weeks' notice, we can get RSVPs in, sort out travel, and actually make it something people are excited about rather than stressed by.

Can I ask — is there a reason tomorrow specifically? If there's a milestone or occasion we're celebrating, that would really help me frame the event and find the right venue. London has some wonderful options depending on the vibe: something classic and a bit formal, or more relaxed and fun. I'll start a shortlist either way and have something for you and Michael to look at within the hour.

London.

Michael wants to hold an office party in *London*. Tomorrow.

I don't know who approved this location change, but it was not me, and it was not run through the Party Planning Committee. The Scranton branch has an established summer party budget of $400 — set June 5th, allocated and approved. A same-day transatlantic event is not a line item. It is not *a* line item. It would consume the entire regional discretionary budget for the quarter, and that assumes we could somehow charter flights, book a venue, arrange catering, and coordinate logistics before close of business today, which we cannot.

My ruling is as follows: **Rejected.** This proposal is non-compliant with the existing approved budget, violates the Party Planning Committee's submission and review process, and is operationally impossible within the stated timeframe. If Michael wishes to hold a summer party in London, he may submit a formal budget proposal to Corporate with no fewer than six weeks' lead time, a detailed vendor plan, and written justification for why Scranton's conference room — which is free — is insufficient.

The approved party remains: Scranton office, $400 budget, white and gold theme, no DJ, no inflatables. Pam may submit expense requests through the standard process. That is what is happening.

Oh my GOD, London?! Okay, okay, okay — this is SUCH a moment, like we are not doing some sad paper company party in a conference room, we are doing LONDON, which means we need to go full elevated British summer because Wimbledon aesthetic is literally having a MAJOR cultural moment right now and if we don't lean into it I will literally cry.

Here's what we're doing: garden party vibes, obviously — strawberries and cream, Pimm's cups and sparkling elderflower for the non-drinkers, a grazing table with those little cucumber sandwiches that look incredible on a platter. We did the Golden Hour Garden Party theme back in June and everyone loved it so we're building on that energy but make it *British*. Think cream and green colour palette, wicker accents, maybe some bunting. It's Pinterest but make it Notting Hill. You want people to feel like they're in a romcom and also a Marks & Spencer ad at the same time.

For music — and I cannot stress this enough — NO classic rock, no one's dad's playlist, no shuffle disasters. We are doing a summer mix: Dua Lipa because she's literally from London and it would be RUDE not to, Beyoncé obviously always, a little Charli XCX because Brat summer is still culturally legible and I will not let it die. Upbeat, feel-good, makes people want to talk to each other. That's the whole science of party music and I learned it by instinct and also by being at a lot of parties.

Ryan would probably try to make it "low-key" and "not a big deal" but Ryan is wrong about everything except that one playlist, so. The vibe is: this is a BIG deal, people should feel special, and tomorrow they're going to tell their friends about it. That's customer experience. That's brand. You're welcome.

Okay. WOW. First of all — London. I said London, and my team *delivered*. That is what happens when you build a family, not just a workforce. Michael Scott, World's Best Boss, dot com.

Here's where we landed, and I want to be clear that this was a *team effort* that I personally inspired: Pam — who is like the little sister I never had, or maybe the receptionist I always wanted, both — has flagged that tomorrow is, logistically speaking, "a lot." Angela, who I respect deeply even though she once gave me a card that just said "no," has pointed out that we have about $400 and that London is not currently in the budget. And Kelly, God bless her, has already planned the most incredible Wimbledon garden party with Pimm's cups and Dua Lipa and I am not entirely sure what a grazing table is but I want twelve of them. So here is the synthesis, the Michael Scott Grand Unified Party Theory: **we are doing London — but not tomorrow.** Tomorrow was a beautiful dream, like when I thought I could be a rapper. The *real* party, the one that people will talk about at their deathbeds, will happen in 2–3 weeks once Pam builds out the full plan, Angela signs off on a revised budget proposal to Corporate, and Kelly finalises the playlist which will absolutely include Beyoncé because, as I have always said, I am Beyoncé, always.

In the meantime, Pam is starting the venue shortlist *right now* — classic and formal OR fun and relaxed, your call — and we just need one piece of information: what are we celebrating? A milestone? A big sale? The fact that we survived another year together as a family? Because that last one is ALWAYS worth a party. Bam. Said the lady.
